urn:contentItem:6JJN-9KW3-RT1Y-C255-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:51ZLexisNexisBUSINESS,INTERNATIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMatthew BrownThe Associated Press

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-N943-S0TN-F3S4-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - RECENT NEWS OF PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - RECENT NEWS OF PEER AND INDUSTRY INSIGHTS

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ PORT MCKENZIE STUDY SIGNALS CROWLEY MARITIME'S PUSH INTO ALASKA FUEL INFRASTRUCTURE

Crowley Maritime has secured permission to study a potential fuel-terminal development at Port McKenzie, a step in line with its Alaska expansion strategy. The January 7, 2026 announcement outlines a feasibility study for the facility, aimed at capitalizing on rising regional interest.The proposed terminal would help establish a reliable fuel supply chain for marine and land operations in Alaska, supporting local industries amid growing energy demand in the region.Strategically located in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough, the project could streamline Crowley's logistics while attracting other businesses and investors to Port McKenzie, strengthening the borough's economic landscape.Approval of the permit enables Crowley to advance studies and engage with stakeholders, potentially signaling a milestone in its Alaska growth plan and future Port McKenzie investments.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME CONSOLIDATES UNITS TO SCALE SHIPPING, LOGISTICS AND ENERGY SOLUTIONS

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 a strategic consolidation creating two divisions: Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler and Energy led by Kollin Fencil, to strengthen execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy arms.The Shipping and Logistics Division merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management into a single unit serving commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transport and warehousing.The Energy Division brings together Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions to drive innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging markets.Both Fowler and Fencil, who joined Crowley as senior vice presidents in 2023, have overseen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the reorganization will enable faster decision-making and greater adaptability to market shifts, helping Crowley maintain its leadership in maritime, energy and logistics.

+ CROWLEY, SEACOR CREATE FAIRWATER HOLDINGS TO EXPAND U.S. PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL TRANSPORT

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings have announced Fairwater Holdings, a new joint venture between the two groups to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transport assets and to operate as a Jones Act service provider.The venture will bring together a fleet of 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers under long-term charters and will also handle crewing and technical management for 21 third-party vessels.Daniel Thorogood, CEO of Seabulk, will become Fairwater's CEO after closing, with Crowley's leadership signaling a focus on safe, efficient maritime transportation for the U.S. energy sector.Closing conditions and regulatory approvals are required before operations begin, which are expected to start in the first quarter of 2024.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ MAWANI BROADENS GLOBAL NETWORK AS CMACGM'S OCEAN RISE BEGINS

RIYADH: Mawani has added CMACGM's Ocean Rise service to Jeddah Islamic Port, expanding connectivity to select Asian and European markets. The service began on 3rd of May 2026, linking Jeddah with Kobe, Nagoya, Yokohama; Xiamen, Yantian, Nansha; Rotterdam; Hamburg; and Southampton.The service can handle up to 3,001 TEUs and supports Saudi trade in coordination with international lines as part of the National Transport and Logistics Strategy to position the Kingdom as a three-continent logistics hub.In the period Mawani launched 18 services with total capacity 123,552 TEUs, and port activity grew with March throughput up 6.66% year on year and February 667,882 TEUs.Jeddah Islamic Port remains a key gateway with the facilities to support international trade.

+ CMA CGM BETS ON INDIA AS CABOTAGE CHANGES PUSH FLEET REGISTRATION

CMACGM has transferred two more containerships into the Indian registry, increasing its Indian-flag fleet to six and following earlier moves of four vessels.The Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt ship built in 2009 with 2,556 TEU capacity, moved from Malta to India, and CMACGM has also filed papers to transfer the Semarang, built in 2007 with 38,000 DWT and 2,700 TEU.The re-flagging aligns with India's cabotage changes that restrict foreign-flagged vessels from carrying domestic cargo, with January 2026 marking the rollback of a transshipment exemption. CMA CGM has started employing Indian seafarers on its Indian-flag ships, aiming for 1,500 by year-end, and in February 2026 signed a contract with Cochin Shipyard for LNG-powered newbuilds.

+ CMA CGM UNVEILS AI-DRIVEN BLUEPRINT FOR SMARTER, MORE RESILIENT SUPPLY CHAINS IN INDIA

Volatility in supply chains and rising customer expectations are prompting a reinvention of logistics. CMA CGM Group depicts India as both a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, with AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms transforming port operations and client experiences. The result is a move from reactive to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM committed to using artificial intelligence to boost operational efficiency in logistics. The focus is on AI-driven tools that optimize supply chain management and reduce disruptions from volatile markets, leveraging analytics and machine learning to build a more resilient network.CMA CGM plans partnerships with Indian tech players to build an ecosystem that supports AI integration in logistics. This collaborative approach speeds innovation and helps position India as a critical global logistics hub. AI is intended to be a core operating element that enables smarter decisions and better service.Additionally, CMACGM is investing in digital platforms that enhance transparency and real-time shipment tracking, empowering businesses to act on insights and improving overall performance. The company aims to create a seamless client experience that supports growth in e-commerce and manufacturing.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

The service began on 3rd of May 2026, linking Jeddah with Kobe, Nagoya, Yokohama; Xiamen, Yantian, Nansha; Rotterdam; Hamburg; and Southampton.The service can handle up to 3,001 TEUs and supports Saudi trade in coordination with international lines as part of the National Transport and Logistics Strategy to position the Kingdom as a three-continent logistics hub.In the period Mawani launched 18 services with total capacity 123,552 TEUs, and port activity grew with March throughput up 6.66% year on year and February 667,882 TEUs.Jeddah Islamic Port remains a key gateway with the facilities to support international trade.

+ CMA CGM BETS ON INDIA AS CABOTAGE CHANGES PUSH FLEET REGISTRATION

CMACGM has transferred two more containerships into the Indian registry, increasing its Indian-flag fleet to six and following earlier moves of four vessels.The Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt ship built in 2009 with 2,556 TEU capacity, moved from Malta to India, and CMACGM has also filed papers to transfer the Semarang, built in 2007 with 38,000 DWT and 2,700 TEU.The re-flagging aligns with India's cabotage changes that restrict foreign-flagged vessels from carrying domestic cargo, with January 2026 marking the rollback of a transshipment exemption. CMA CGM has started employing Indian seafarers on its Indian-flag ships, aiming for 1,500 by year-end, and in February 2026 signed a contract with Cochin Shipyard for LNG-powered newbuilds.

+ CMA CGM UNVEILS AI-DRIVEN BLUEPRINT FOR SMARTER, MORE RESILIENT SUPPLY CHAINS IN INDIA

Volatility in supply chains and rising customer expectations are prompting a reinvention of logistics. CMA CGM Group depicts India as both a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, with AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms transforming port operations and client experiences. The result is a move from reactive to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM committed to using artificial intelligence to boost operational efficiency in logistics. The focus is on AI-driven tools that optimize supply chain management and reduce disruptions from volatile markets, leveraging analytics and machine learning to build a more resilient network.CMA CGM plans partnerships with Indian tech players to build an ecosystem that supports AI integration in logistics. This collaborative approach speeds innovation and helps position India as a critical global logistics hub. AI is intended to be a core operating element that enables smarter decisions and better service.Additionally, CMACGM is investing in digital platforms that enhance transparency and real-time shipment tracking, empowering businesses to act on insights and improving overall performance. The company aims to create a seamless client experience that supports growth in e-commerce and manufacturing.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

urn:contentItem:6JHR-SDG3-RRVD-717K-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - RECENT INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS ROUNDUP2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - RECENT INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS ROUNDUP

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ PORT MCKENZIE STUDY SIGNALS CROWLEY MARITIME'S PUSH INTO ALASKA FUEL INFRASTRUCTURE

Crowley Maritime has secured permission to study a potential fuel-terminal development at Port McKenzie, a step in line with its Alaska expansion strategy. The January 7, 2026 announcement outlines a feasibility study for the facility, aimed at capitalizing on rising regional interest.The proposed terminal would help establish a reliable fuel supply chain for marine and land operations in Alaska, supporting local industries amid growing energy demand in the region.Strategically located in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough, the project could streamline Crowley's logistics while attracting other businesses and investors to Port McKenzie, strengthening the borough's economic landscape.Approval of the permit enables Crowley to advance studies and engage with stakeholders, potentially signaling a milestone in its Alaska growth plan and future Port McKenzie investments.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME CONSOLIDATES UNITS TO SCALE SHIPPING, LOGISTICS AND ENERGY SOLUTIONS

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 a strategic consolidation creating two divisions: Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler and Energy led by Kollin Fencil, to strengthen execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy arms.The Shipping and Logistics Division merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management into a single unit serving commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transport and warehousing.The Energy Division brings together Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions to drive innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging markets.Both Fowler and Fencil, who joined Crowley as senior vice presidents in 2023, have overseen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the reorganization will enable faster decision-making and greater adaptability to market shifts, helping Crowley maintain its leadership in maritime, energy and logistics.

+ CROWLEY, SEACOR CREATE FAIRWATER HOLDINGS TO EXPAND U.S. PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL TRANSPORT

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings have announced Fairwater Holdings, a new joint venture between the two groups to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transport assets and to operate as a Jones Act service provider.The venture will bring together a fleet of 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers under long-term charters and will also handle crewing and technical management for 21 third-party vessels.Daniel Thorogood, CEO of Seabulk, will become Fairwater's CEO after closing, with Crowley's leadership signaling a focus on safe, efficient maritime transportation for the U.S. energy sector.Closing conditions and regulatory approvals are required before operations begin, which are expected to start in the first quarter of 2024.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ MAWANI BROADENS GLOBAL NETWORK AS CMACGM'S OCEAN RISE BEGINS

The service began on 3rd of May 2026, linking Jeddah with Kobe, Nagoya, Yokohama; Xiamen, Yantian, Nansha; Rotterdam; Hamburg; and Southampton.The service can handle up to 3,001 TEUs and supports Saudi trade in coordination with international lines as part of the National Transport and Logistics Strategy to position the Kingdom as a three-continent logistics hub.In the period Mawani launched 18 services with total capacity 123,552 TEUs, and port activity grew with March throughput up 6.66% year on year and February 667,882 TEUs.Jeddah Islamic Port remains a key gateway with the facilities to support international trade.

+ CMA CGM BETS ON INDIA AS CABOTAGE CHANGES PUSH FLEET REGISTRATION

CMACGM has transferred two more containerships into the Indian registry, increasing its Indian-flag fleet to six and following earlier moves of four vessels.The Vila do Conde, a 33,434-dwt ship built in 2009 with 2,556 TEU capacity, moved from Malta to India, and CMACGM has also filed papers to transfer the Semarang, built in 2007 with 38,000 DWT and 2,700 TEU.The re-flagging aligns with India's cabotage changes that restrict foreign-flagged vessels from carrying domestic cargo, with January 2026 marking the rollback of a transshipment exemption. CMA CGM has started employing Indian seafarers on its Indian-flag ships, aiming for 1,500 by year-end, and in February 2026 signed a contract with Cochin Shipyard for LNG-powered newbuilds.

+ CMA CGM UNVEILS AI-DRIVEN BLUEPRINT FOR SMARTER, MORE RESILIENT SUPPLY CHAINS IN INDIA

Volatility in supply chains and rising customer expectations are prompting a reinvention of logistics. CMA CGM Group depicts India as both a strategic growth market and a global innovation hub, with AI, ecosystem partnerships, and digital platforms transforming port operations and client experiences. The result is a move from reactive to predictive, intelligent, and increasingly autonomous supply chains.On 28th April 2026, CMA CGM committed to using artificial intelligence to boost operational efficiency in logistics. The focus is on AI-driven tools that optimize supply chain management and reduce disruptions from volatile markets, leveraging analytics and machine learning to build a more resilient network.CMA CGM plans partnerships with Indian tech players to build an ecosystem that supports AI integration in logistics. urn:contentItem:6JHH-T8D3-S949-V3G1-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - INDUSTRY AND PEER INSIGHTS: UP-TO-DATE2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - INDUSTRY AND PEER INSIGHTS: UP-TO-DATE

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ PORT MCKENZIE STUDY SIGNALS CROWLEY MARITIME'S PUSH INTO ALASKA FUEL INFRASTRUCTURE

Crowley Maritime has secured permission to study a potential fuel-terminal development at Port McKenzie, a step in line with its Alaska expansion strategy. The January 7, 2026 announcement outlines a feasibility study for the facility, aimed at capitalizing on rising regional interest.The proposed terminal would help establish a reliable fuel supply chain for marine and land operations in Alaska, supporting local industries amid growing energy demand in the region.Strategically located in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough, the project could streamline Crowley's logistics while attracting other businesses and investors to Port McKenzie, strengthening the borough's economic landscape.Approval of the permit enables Crowley to advance studies and engage with stakeholders, potentially signaling a milestone in its Alaska growth plan and future Port McKenzie investments.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME CONSOLIDATES UNITS TO SCALE SHIPPING, LOGISTICS AND ENERGY SOLUTIONS

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 a strategic consolidation creating two divisions: Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler and Energy led by Kollin Fencil, to strengthen execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy arms.The Shipping and Logistics Division merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management into a single unit serving commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transport and warehousing.The Energy Division brings together Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions to drive innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging markets.Both Fowler and Fencil, who joined Crowley as senior vice presidents in 2023, have overseen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the reorganization will enable faster decision-making and greater adaptability to market shifts, helping Crowley maintain its leadership in maritime, energy and logistics.

+ CROWLEY, SEACOR CREATE FAIRWATER HOLDINGS TO EXPAND U.S. PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL TRANSPORT

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings have announced Fairwater Holdings, a new joint venture between the two groups to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transport assets and to operate as a Jones Act service provider.The venture will bring together a fleet of 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers under long-term charters and will also handle crewing and technical management for 21 third-party vessels.Daniel Thorogood, CEO of Seabulk, will become Fairwater's CEO after closing, with Crowley's leadership signaling a focus on safe, efficient maritime transportation for the U.S. energy sector.Closing conditions and regulatory approvals are required before operations begin, which are expected to start in the first quarter of 2024.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ CMA CGM ADVANCES INDIA STRATEGY WITH FIFTH VESSEL REFLAGGING

MUMBAI: CMA CGM Group has reflagged its fifth container vessel in India, with the date 24th April 2026 signaling a continued push to improve service efficiency on major routes. The Vila do Conde is now registered under the Indian flag, a step that aligns with India's plan to grow domestic-flag tonnage and reduce reliance on foreign-flag ships.The company has already completed five reflaggings since last year and is targeting a sixth by the end of May as part of its expansion strategy. In February, CMA CGM ordered six dual-fuel vessels from Cochin Shipyard for about $350 million and outlined a $300 million investment in logistics infrastructure in Maharashtra. Additionally, CMACGM is exploring opportunities in inland waterways and has discussed using GIFT City as a vessel-registration hub to support maritime security and competitiveness.

+ GEMALINK EXPANSION BY CMA CGM SIGNALS STRONG COMMITMENT TO VIETNAM'S MARITIME HUB AMBITIONS

CMA CGM Group has begun Phase 2 expansion at the Gemalink container terminal in Cai Mep port, southern Vietnam, after starting on 20th April 2026, aiming to bolster CMACGM's Southeast Asia port capacity. When complete, capacity will rise from 1.7m TEUs to about 3m TEUs; the quay will gain 450 meters, the yard will grow from 32 to 44 hectares, and five new cranes will be added, for a total of thirteen. Gemalink opened in 2021 and now handles 1.7m TEUs annually, underscoring its role as a key node in CMACGM's regional logistics network along major routes. The expansion is part of CMA CGM's broader effort to deepen its footprint in Vietnam as the country aims to become a leading maritime logistics hub by mid-century.

+ PORT OF CORK BENEFITS FROM CMA CGM'S STRENGTHENED TRANSATLANTIC PAD SERVICE

CMA CGM Group is expanding its Transatlantic PAD service with Cork added to the route, effective from 28th April 2026.The upgrade includes direct weekly calls to New York and Savannah with transit times around nine days and eleven days respectively; an additional vessel and optimized rotations are intended to improve schedule reliability.Direct calls at Dunkerque and Zeebrugge plus connections to Le Havre and Rotterdam enable feeder movements to Baltic and Scandinavian markets; Port Liberty terminals in the United States provide priority berthing to reduce congestion impact.The service will carry pharmaceuticals, beverages, and general cargo, appealing to high value and time-sensitive shipments and offering a viable alternative to air freight. CMA CGM has operated in Ireland for more than two decades through three ports and up to six weekly sailings, connecting Ireland to over 420 ports worldwide and supporting sectors including pharmaceuticals, agri-food, and manufacturing.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

urn:contentItem:6JHB-0NR3-RVT7-63FW-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - INDUSTRY AND PEER TRENDS: THE RECENT2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - INDUSTRY AND PEER TRENDS: THE RECENT

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ PORT MCKENZIE STUDY SIGNALS CROWLEY MARITIME'S PUSH INTO ALASKA FUEL INFRASTRUCTURE

Crowley Maritime has secured permission to study a potential fuel-terminal development at Port McKenzie, a step in line with its Alaska expansion strategy. The January 7, 2026 announcement outlines a feasibility study for the facility, aimed at capitalizing on rising regional interest.The proposed terminal would help establish a reliable fuel supply chain for marine and land operations in Alaska, supporting local industries amid growing energy demand in the region.Strategically located in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough, the project could streamline Crowley's logistics while attracting other businesses and investors to Port McKenzie, strengthening the borough's economic landscape.Approval of the permit enables Crowley to advance studies and engage with stakeholders, potentially signaling a milestone in its Alaska growth plan and future Port McKenzie investments.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME CONSOLIDATES UNITS TO SCALE SHIPPING, LOGISTICS AND ENERGY SOLUTIONS

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 a strategic consolidation creating two divisions: Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler and Energy led by Kollin Fencil, to strengthen execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy arms.The Shipping and Logistics Division merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management into a single unit serving commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transport and warehousing.The Energy Division brings together Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions to drive innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging markets.Both Fowler and Fencil, who joined Crowley as senior vice presidents in 2023, have overseen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the reorganization will enable faster decision-making and greater adaptability to market shifts, helping Crowley maintain its leadership in maritime, energy and logistics.

+ CROWLEY, SEACOR CREATE FAIRWATER HOLDINGS TO EXPAND U.S. PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL TRANSPORT

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings have announced Fairwater Holdings, a new joint venture between the two groups to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transport assets and to operate as a Jones Act service provider.The venture will bring together a fleet of 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers under long-term charters and will also handle crewing and technical management for 21 third-party vessels.Daniel Thorogood, CEO of Seabulk, will become Fairwater's CEO after closing, with Crowley's leadership signaling a focus on safe, efficient maritime transportation for the U.S. energy sector.Closing conditions and regulatory approvals are required before operations begin, which are expected to start in the first quarter of 2024.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ CMA CGM ADVANCES INDIA STRATEGY WITH FIFTH VESSEL REFLAGGING

MUMBAI: CMA CGM Group has reflagged its fifth container vessel in India, with the date 24th April 2026 signaling a continued push to improve service efficiency on major routes. The Vila do Conde is now registered under the Indian flag, a step that aligns with India's plan to grow domestic-flag tonnage and reduce reliance on foreign-flag ships.The company has already completed five reflaggings since last year and is targeting a sixth by the end of May as part of its expansion strategy. In February, CMA CGM ordered six dual-fuel vessels from Cochin Shipyard for about $350 million and outlined a $300 million investment in logistics infrastructure in Maharashtra. Additionally, CMACGM is exploring opportunities in inland waterways and has discussed using GIFT City as a vessel-registration hub to support maritime security and competitiveness.

+ GEMALINK EXPANSION BY CMA CGM SIGNALS STRONG COMMITMENT TO VIETNAM'S MARITIME HUB AMBITIONS

CMA CGM Group has begun Phase 2 expansion at the Gemalink container terminal in Cai Mep port, southern Vietnam, after starting on 20th April 2026, aiming to bolster CMACGM's Southeast Asia port capacity. When complete, capacity will rise from 1.7m TEUs to about 3m TEUs; the quay will gain 450 meters, the yard will grow from 32 to 44 hectares, and five new cranes will be added, for a total of thirteen. Gemalink opened in 2021 and now handles 1.7m TEUs annually, underscoring its role as a key node in CMACGM's regional logistics network along major routes. The expansion is part of CMA CGM's broader effort to deepen its footprint in Vietnam as the country aims to become a leading maritime logistics hub by mid-century.

+ PORT OF CORK BENEFITS FROM CMA CGM'S STRENGTHENED TRANSATLANTIC PAD SERVICE

CMA CGM Group is expanding its Transatlantic PAD service with Cork added to the route, effective from 28th April 2026.The upgrade includes direct weekly calls to New York and Savannah with transit times around nine days and eleven days respectively; an additional vessel and optimized rotations are intended to improve schedule reliability.Direct calls at Dunkerque and Zeebrugge plus connections to Le Havre and Rotterdam enable feeder movements to Baltic and Scandinavian markets; Port Liberty terminals in the United States provide priority berthing to reduce congestion impact.The service will carry pharmaceuticals, beverages, and general cargo, appealing to high value and time-sensitive shipments and offering a viable alternative to air freight. CMA CGM has operated in Ireland for more than two decades through three ports and up to six weekly sailings, connecting Ireland to over 420 ports worldwide and supporting sectors including pharmaceuticals, agri-food, and manufacturing.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

ACTIVITIES:

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

urn:contentItem:6JGF-YBS3-S2WN-K0MS-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY TRENDS2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY TRENDS

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ PORT MCKENZIE STUDY SIGNALS CROWLEY MARITIME'S PUSH INTO ALASKA FUEL INFRASTRUCTURE

Crowley Maritime has secured permission to study a potential fuel-terminal development at Port McKenzie, a step in line with its Alaska expansion strategy. The January 7, 2026 announcement outlines a feasibility study for the facility, aimed at capitalizing on rising regional interest.The proposed terminal would help establish a reliable fuel supply chain for marine and land operations in Alaska, supporting local industries amid growing energy demand in the region.Strategically located in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough, the project could streamline Crowley's logistics while attracting other businesses and investors to Port McKenzie, strengthening the borough's economic landscape.Approval of the permit enables Crowley to advance studies and engage with stakeholders, potentially signaling a milestone in its Alaska growth plan and future Port McKenzie investments.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME CONSOLIDATES UNITS TO SCALE SHIPPING, LOGISTICS AND ENERGY SOLUTIONS

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 a strategic consolidation creating two divisions: Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler and Energy led by Kollin Fencil, to strengthen execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy arms.The Shipping and Logistics Division merges Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management into a single unit serving commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transport and warehousing.The Energy Division brings together Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions to drive innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging markets.Both Fowler and Fencil, who joined Crowley as senior vice presidents in 2023, have overseen improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the reorganization will enable faster decision-making and greater adaptability to market shifts, helping Crowley maintain its leadership in maritime, energy and logistics.

+ CROWLEY, SEACOR CREATE FAIRWATER HOLDINGS TO EXPAND U.S. PETROLEUM AND CHEMICAL TRANSPORT

Crowley Maritime and SEACOR Holdings have announced Fairwater Holdings, a new joint venture between the two groups to integrate their liquid energy and chemical transport assets and to operate as a Jones Act service provider.The venture will bring together a fleet of 20 ocean-going articulated tug-barges and 11 tankers under long-term charters and will also handle crewing and technical management for 21 third-party vessels.Daniel Thorogood, CEO of Seabulk, will become Fairwater's CEO after closing, with Crowley's leadership signaling a focus on safe, efficient maritime transportation for the U.S. energy sector.Closing conditions and regulatory approvals are required before operations begin, which are expected to start in the first quarter of 2024.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ CMA CGM ADVANCES INDIA STRATEGY WITH FIFTH VESSEL REFLAGGING

MUMBAI: CMA CGM Group has reflagged its fifth container vessel in India, with the date 24th April 2026 signaling a continued push to improve service efficiency on major routes. The Vila do Conde is now registered under the Indian flag, a step that aligns with India's plan to grow domestic-flag tonnage and reduce reliance on foreign-flag ships.The company has already completed five reflaggings since last year and is targeting a sixth by the end of May as part of its expansion strategy. In February, CMA CGM ordered six dual-fuel vessels from Cochin Shipyard for about $350 million and outlined a $300 million investment in logistics infrastructure in Maharashtra. Additionally, CMACGM is exploring opportunities in inland waterways and has discussed using GIFT City as a vessel-registration hub to support maritime security and competitiveness.

+ GEMALINK EXPANSION BY CMA CGM SIGNALS STRONG COMMITMENT TO VIETNAM'S MARITIME HUB AMBITIONS

CMA CGM Group has begun Phase 2 expansion at the Gemalink container terminal in Cai Mep port, southern Vietnam, after starting on 20th April 2026, aiming to bolster CMACGM's Southeast Asia port capacity. When complete, capacity will rise from 1.7m TEUs to about 3m TEUs; the quay will gain 450 meters, the yard will grow from 32 to 44 hectares, and five new cranes will be added, for a total of thirteen. Gemalink opened in 2021 and now handles 1.7m TEUs annually, underscoring its role as a key node in CMACGM's regional logistics network along major routes. The expansion is part of CMA CGM's broader effort to deepen its footprint in Vietnam as the country aims to become a leading maritime logistics hub by mid-century.

+ PORT OF CORK BENEFITS FROM CMA CGM'S STRENGTHENED TRANSATLANTIC PAD SERVICE

CMA CGM Group is expanding its Transatlantic PAD service with Cork added to the route, effective from 28th April 2026.The upgrade includes direct weekly calls to New York and Savannah with transit times around nine days and eleven days respectively; an additional vessel and optimized rotations are intended to improve schedule reliability.Direct calls at Dunkerque and Zeebrugge plus connections to Le Havre and Rotterdam enable feeder movements to Baltic and Scandinavian markets; Port Liberty terminals in the United States provide priority berthing to reduce congestion impact.The service will carry pharmaceuticals, beverages, and general cargo, appealing to high value and time-sensitive shipments and offering a viable alternative to air freight. CMA CGM has operated in Ireland for more than two decades through three ports and up to six weekly sailings, connecting Ireland to over 420 ports worldwide and supporting sectors including pharmaceuticals, agri-food, and manufacturing.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CSY3-SCTW-62K4-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CTM3-SCTW-63FD-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CW13-SD69-R46P-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JK4-WBY3-S0GM-T14F-00000-00CROWLEY MARITIME - COMPLETE UPDATES ON INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisSTOCKCROWLEY MARITIME - COMPLETE UPDATES ON INDUSTRY AND PEER NEWS

DASHBOARD: CROWLEY MARITIME

PermID4295904276Websitehttp://www.crowley.com/IndustryMaritime & Shipping Logistics

ACTIVITIES:

Crowley Maritime Corporation, founded in 1892, is a family-owned logistics and maritime services company headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. Employing over 5,300 people, it operates in shipping, energy, and supply chain management, generating over $2 billion annually. Crowley provides ocean transport, LNG distribution, and logistics for government and commercial clients. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy logistics, supporting offshore wind projects. Its culture emphasizes safety, innovation, and sustainability, with robust employee training. Competing with Maersk, Crowley stands out for its U.S.-focused operations and government contracts. Recent initiatives include decarbonization efforts and digital tracking systems, aligning with green logistics trends.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME [3 News That Matters]

+ CS ANTHEM PASSES STRAIT OF HORMUZ UNDER U.S. ESCORT, CROWLEY SAYS

Crowley Maritime confirmed that its managed tanker CS Anthem completed a transit of the Strait of Hormuz, providing visibility into the small cadre of U.S.-flagged ships that have left the Persian Gulf since the latest crisis began. The company said the transit occurred on the 5th of May, 2026.Crowley stressed that crew safety and continuity of service remain top priorities and credited the professionalism of U.S. Navy and government partners supporting maritime safety. It declined to disclose further operational details, citing company policy against discussing vessel routing or security arrangements.CS Anthem is one of two U.S.-flagged commercial vessels confirmed to have transited Hormuz under the Trump administration's newly initiated 'Project Freedom' operation, which aims to evacuate ships via Oman’s territorial waters within an enhanced security area. The other is A.P. Moller–Maersk's Alliance Fairfax, also exited the Persian Gulf under U.S. military escort.Despite these successes, the broader Strait of Hormuz shipping environment remains precarious, with risks from mines, drone activity, and a larger military footprint deterring a broader return of commercial traffic. Observers describe the movements as highly coordinated, one-off transits rather than a signal of a broad reopening.Both the CS Anthem and Alliance Fairfax belong to the Maritime Security Program (MSP) or the Tanker Security Program (TSP), ensuring the availability of U.S.-flagged vessels for military logistics during crises. The CS Anthem is a 49,990-metric-ton medium-range tanker managed by Crowley under a bareboat charter, illustrating the strategic role of Crowley in this complex maritime landscape.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME ADVANCES ALASKA GROWTH WITH PORT MCKENZIE FUEL TERMINAL FEASIBILITY

Crowley Maritime has announced a plan to conduct a feasibility study for a potential fuel terminal at Port McKenzie, signaling a broader push to expand its Alaska footprint. The permit was granted on January 7, 2026, as rising regional activity underscores demand for improved logistics and transportation capabilities.Officials say the terminal would provide a reliable fuel-supply chain for local marine and land operations, supporting growing commercial activity in the Matanuska-Susitna Borough and the broader Alaska economy.Crowley's strategy centers on leveraging Port McKenzie's location to enhance logistics and attract other tenants. If developed, the project could streamline Crowley's operations and help catalyze additional investment in the area.With the permit in place, Crowley moves forward with feasibility assessments and engagement with local stakeholders, marking a potential milestone for the company and for Port McKenzie as they explore future growth opportunities.

+ CROWLEY MARITIME ANNOUNCES DUAL-DIVISION REALIGNMENT TO ENHANCE OPERATIONAL AGILITY

Crowley Maritime announced on January 6, 2026 the formation of two new divisions—Shipping and Logistics led by James C. Fowler, and Energy led by Kollin Fencil—as part of a strategic consolidation to improve execution and accelerate growth across its shipping, logistics and energy activities.The Shipping and Logistics Division combines Crowley's logistics, land transportation and global ship management to serve commercial and government customers with ocean shipping, inland trucking, multimodal transportation and warehousing.Fowler said the reorganized structure will enable cohesive management of the entire transportation value chain, driving growth through safe and efficient supply-chain solutions.The Energy Division consolidates Crowley Fuels in Alaska, LNG services and advanced energy solutions, enabling greater innovation and scale in both traditional and emerging energy markets.Fencil noted the realignment will help Crowley meet evolving customer demands for traditional fuels and advanced energy solutions, and the leaders have delivered improvements in financial performance and operational efficiency since joining the company in 2023. Chief Operating Officer Megan Davidson said the realignment will speed decision-making and improve the company's ability to respond to market dynamics, reinforcing Crowley's status as a leader in maritime, energy and logistics. Crowley is privately held and U.S.-owned, with a footprint spanning 36 nations and territories and more than 130 years in business.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ CMA CGM LOGISTICS [3 News That Matters]

+ CMA CGM COMMITS €700 MILLION TO MODERNIZE KENYA'S MOMBASA TERMINALS AMID AFRICA EXPANSION

French shipping and logistics group CMACGM has signed a strategic partnership with the Government of Kenya to expand the country’s transport and logistics infrastructure. The agreement, finalized on 11th May 2026 during the Africa Forward Summit, was witnessed by President William Ruto and French President Emmanuel Macron, and seeks to bolster the development of port capacity, inland logistics, and freight management systems in Kenya and the wider East and Central African region.The partnership aims to identify investment opportunities to enhance Kenya’s logistics ecosystem and strengthen regional integration into global trade routes through upgraded infrastructure. Macron announced CMA CGM has committed 700 million euros toward the renovation of two terminals at the Port of Mombasa, a central element of this broader logistics and transport partnership.CMACGM said its response to rising maritime trade and supply chain demands in East and Central Africa includes improving connectivity between ports, inland corridors, and international markets. The company noted its Kenyan operations date back to 2005, connecting Mombasa and Lamu to global markets and supporting inland transport corridors across East Africa.CMA CGM’s Africa strategy prioritises expanding port and inland infrastructure, enhancing logistics integration, and supporting decarbonisation of transport systems. It currently manages nine container terminals across Africa and is investing in both maritime and land-based logistics hubs to boost trade efficiency and regional connectivity.Beyond infrastructure, CMACGM highlighted social investments in Kenya through the CMA CGM Foundation, including expanding the I.O. Me001 Innovation Center in Mombasa and renovating the University of Nairobi’s main football field. The group, headquartered in Marseille, operates in 177 countries with a fleet of more than 700 vessels, and transported over 24 million containers globally in 2025.

+ CMA CGM SIGNS KENYA FRAMEWORK TO EXPAND PORT, INLAND CAPACITY

CMA CGM Group has signed a high-level framework agreement with Kenyan authorities to advance Kenya's logistics and port capacity, a move unveiled during the Africa Forward Summit in the presence of the Presidents of the French and Kenyan Republics, M. Emmanuel Macron and M. William Ruto.The framework is designed to accelerate solutions that support the growth of supply chain flows in East and Central Africa, covering maritime demand, inland logistics, and freight management, and aims to improve efficiency and resilience across the region.CMA CGM has been active in Kenya since 2005, connecting the ports of Mombasa and Lamu to global markets and facilitating inland corridors throughout East Africa.With a presence on the African continent for more than 50 years, CMACGM pursues a long-term development strategy focused on securing port and inland capacities, developing regional hubs, and integrating logistics solutions, while also accelerating the decarbonization of transport chains.The group currently operates and develops nine container terminals across Africa, including the Kribi Container Terminal in Cameroon, where CMACGM is a major stakeholder, and the Lekki Deep Sea Port in Nigeria, central to its Gulf of Guinea strategy as a gateway for West Africa.Beyond infrastructure, CMA CGM emphasizes social responsibility, with the CMA CGM Foundation announcing two projects in Kenya aimed at strengthening education and innovation, including the PharmaBoxes initiative to provide mobile pharmacies and the Containers of Hope program delivering humanitarian aid globally.

+ CMA CGM EXPANDS REGIONAL FOOTPRINT IN EAST AFRICA THROUGH KENYAN COLLABORATION

At the Africa Forward Summit in Nairobi on 11th-12th May 2026, CMACGM and the Government of Kenya signed a strategic partnership to bolster transport and logistics infrastructure, including port capacity, inland logistics and freight management. The ceremony was attended by French President Emmanuel Macron and Kenyan President William Ruto.The partners said the agreement will explore joint investments in Kenya's logistics and port infrastructure to accommodate growing supply chain flows into and out of East and Central Africa.CMACGM noted that the initiative will enhance the region's connections to global trade routes and reinforce its commitment to regional development.The move builds on CMACGM's existing footprint in Kenya, where it has operated since 2005, linking Mombasa and Lamu with global markets and inland corridors across East Africa. The Kenyan agreement will integrate the country into CMACGM's broader African network, which already includes nine container terminals on the continent.The group's longer term Africa strategy centers on securing port and inland capacity, developing regional hubs, integrating logistics services and decarbonizing transport chains. Current CMA CGM projects include expanding the Kribi Container Terminal in Cameroon, developing Lekki Deep Sea Port in Nigeria and plans for a deep-water terminal in Pointe-Noire, Republic ofCongo. It also emphasizes multimodal corridors through its CEVA Logistics subsidiary to connect maritime, rail and road transport and improve reliability and transit times.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

urn:contentItem:6JJK-HGD3-RVPP-N13J-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisNEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWN

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees...

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land...

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.Related video above: The Trump Administration is working to displace 900 bison in Montana, after authorizing grazing permits during his first term.But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states, including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration has also sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles. The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and, for more than a century, has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases. BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. Related video above: The Trump Administration is working to displace 900 bison in Montana, after authorizing grazing permits during his first term. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states, including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration has also sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles. The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and, for more than a century, has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

The rule allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. By MATTHEW BROWN BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump’s administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department’s Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands meaning restoration leases to a position of prominence. The federal government’s vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden’s administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-BRM3-SCMM-W0TB-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:50ZLexisNexisNEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMatthew Brown

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land....

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing...

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.
